Do: Create modules for repeated patterns, use registry modules
Code Example Good: module "vpc" { source = "./modules/vpc" cidr_block = var.vpc_cidr environment = var.environment }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_vpc" "main" { cidr_block = "10.0.0.0/16" } # Repeated in every project

Keywords: file, structure, organization, main, variables, outputs
Do: Use standard file structure: main.tf, variables.tf, outputs.tf, providers.tf
Don't: Put everything in one large file
Code Example Good: # Standard structure: # main.tf - resources # variables.tf - inputs # outputs.tf - outputs # providers.tf - provider config # versions.tf - version constraints
Code Example Bad: # Bad: Everything in main.tf # 2000 lines of mixed resources, variables, outputs

Description: Use locals for computed and repeated values
Do: Define computed values in locals block
Code Example Good: locals { common_tags = { Environment = var.environment Project = var.project ManagedBy = "terraform" } } resource "aws_instance" "web" { tags = local.common_tags }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_instance" "web" { tags = { Environment = var.environment Project = var.project } } # Repeated everywhere

Do: Include description and type for every variable
Code Example Good: variable "environment" { type = string description = "Environment name (dev, staging, prod)" validation { condition = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment) error_message = "Environment must be dev, staging, or prod." } }
Code Example Bad: variable "environment" { } # No type, description, or validation

Do: Provide defaults for optional variables, require critical ones
Code Example Good: variable "instance_type" { type = string default = "t3.micro" description = "EC2 instance type" } variable "vpc_id" { type = string description = "VPC ID - required" # No default = required }
Code Example Bad: variable "environment" { default = "prod" # Dangerous default } variable "instance_count" { # No default for optional value }

Do: Use validation blocks to catch errors early
Code Example Good: variable "environment" { type = string validation { condition = contains(["dev", "staging", "prod"], var.environment) error_message = "Valid values: dev, staging, prod." } } variable "cidr_block" { type = string validation { condition = can(cidrhost(var.cidr_block, 0)) error_message = "Must be valid CIDR notation." } }
Code Example Bad: variable "environment" { type = string # Any value accepted, errors at apply time }

Do: Output IDs, ARNs, endpoints needed by other configs
Code Example Good: output "vpc_id" { value = aws_vpc.main.id description = "VPC ID for use in other modules" } output "private_subnet_ids" { value = aws_subnet.private[*].id description = "List of private subnet IDs" }
Code Example Bad: output "vpc" { value = aws_vpc.main # Entire resource - too much }

Description: Pin provider versions to prevent breaking changes
Do: Use version constraints in required_providers
Code Example Good: terraform { required_providers { aws = { source = "hashicorp/aws" version = "~> 5.0" } } required_version = ">= 1.5.0" }
Code Example Bad: terraform { required_providers { aws = { source = "hashicorp/aws" # No version - uses latest } } }

Description: Pin module versions in module source
Do: Specify version for registry modules, commit hash for git
Code Example Good: module "vpc" { source = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws" version = "5.0.0" }
Code Example Bad: module "vpc" { source = "terraform-aws-modules/vpc/aws" # No version - uses latest }

Do: Follow pattern: project-env-resource-identifier
Code Example Good: resource "aws_instance" "myproject_prod_web_001" { tags = { Name = "myproject-prod-web-001" } }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_instance" "instance1" { tags = { Name = "my server" } }

Do: Use snake_case for resource names, variables, outputs
Code Example Good: resource "aws_instance" "web_server" { } variable "instance_count" { } output "vpc_id" { }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_instance" "webServer" { } variable "instanceCount" { }

Keywords: tags, mandatory, cost, owner, environment, compliance
Description: Apply mandatory tags to all resources
Do: Include Environment, Project, Owner, ManagedBy tags
Code Example Good: locals { mandatory_tags = { Environment = var.environment Project = var.project Owner = var.owner ManagedBy = "terraform" CostCenter = var.cost_center } } resource "aws_instance" "web" { tags = merge(local.mandatory_tags, { Name = "web-server" }) }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_instance" "web" { tags = { Name = "web" } # Missing mandatory tags }

Description: Use provider default_tags for automatic tagging
Do: Configure default_tags in AWS provider
Don't: Manually add same tags to every resource
Code Example Good: provider "aws" { region = var.region default_tags { tags = { Environment = var.environment ManagedBy = "terraform" Project = var.project } } }
Code Example Bad: # Adding same tags to every resource manually resource "aws_instance" "web" { tags = { Environment = var.environment ManagedBy = "terraform" } } resource "aws_s3_bucket" "data" { tags = { Environment = var.environment ManagedBy = "terraform" } }

Description: Use data sources to reference existing resources
Do: Use data sources for resources managed elsewhere
Code Example Good: data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" { most_recent = true owners = ["099720109477"] filter { name = "name" values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-*-22.04-amd64-server-*"] } } resource "aws_instance" "web" { ami = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_instance" "web" { ami = "ami-0123456789abcdef0" # Hardcoded, region-specific }

Do: Use implicit dependencies through references
Code Example Good: # Implicit dependency (preferred) resource "aws_instance" "web" { subnet_id = aws_subnet.main.id # Implicit dependency } # Explicit only when needed resource "aws_instance" "web" { depends_on = [aws_iam_role_policy_attachment.web] # No direct reference }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_instance" "web" { depends_on = [ aws_vpc.main, aws_subnet.main, aws_security_group.web, ] # Unnecessary explicit dependencies }

Description: Use lifecycle rules to control resource behavior
Do: Use prevent_destroy for critical resources, ignore_changes for external modifications
Don't: Ignore lifecycle management for important resources
Code Example Good: resource "aws_db_instance" "main" { lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true } } resource "aws_instance" "web" { lifecycle { ignore_changes = [tags["LastModified"]] # External automation } }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_db_instance" "main" { # No protection - could be accidentally destroyed }

Description: Prefer for_each over count for resources
Do: Use for_each with maps/sets for stable addressing
Code Example Good: resource "aws_subnet" "private" { for_each = toset(var.availability_zones) availability_zone = each.value cidr_block = cidrsubnet(var.vpc_cidr, 8, index(var.availability_zones, each.value)) }
Code Example Bad: resource "aws_subnet" "private" { count = length(var.availability_zones) availability_zone = var.availability_zones[count.index] # Index changes cause recreation }

Do: Run terraform fmt -recursive on all code
Code Example Good: # Format all files terraform fmt -recursive # Check formatting in CI terraform fmt -check -recursive
Code Example Bad: # Inconsistent formatting resource "aws_instance""web" { ami="ami-123" instance_type = "t3.micro" }

Do: Include terraform validate in CI pipeline
Code Example Good: # CI pipeline steps - terraform init - terraform validate - terraform plan
Code Example Bad: # Skipping validation # terraform plan # May fail with confusing errors

Do: Use terraform-docs to auto-generate module docs
Code Example Good: # Generate docs terraform-docs markdown table . > README.md # Or use pre-commit hook # .pre-commit-config.yaml repos: - repo: https://github.com/terraform-docs/terraform-docs
Code Example Bad: # Manually written docs that get out of sync # README.md - Variables: (outdated list)
